key_insight: A urinary-metabolite machine-learning score (uLCI) using four metabolites plus age, race, and smoking can non-invasively detect lung cancer and track risk across stages.
clinical_relevance: If validated further, uLCI could complement LDCT screening by offering an interpretable urine-based risk test with potential use beyond smoking-eligible populations.
method: Lasso-regularized logistic regression combined creatine riboside, N-acetylneuraminic acid, 27-nor-5β-cholestane-3,7,12,24,25-pentol, cortisol sulfate, and clinical variables.
Developed in the NCI-MD cohort with 10-fold cross-validation and tested without refitting in an independent Colorado cohort.
Evaluated discrimination, stage-wise trends, reclassification, and post-resection survival association.
evidence_signal: uLCI achieved AUC 0.906 in development and 0.748 in independent validation, with monotonic stage-wise increases and preserved stage-specific discrimination.
watch_item: Performance dropped in external validation, so broader prospective testing, calibration review, and real-world implementation studies are still needed.
paper_title: The urinary-metabolite-based lung cancer index (uLCI): an interpretable machine-learning risk model for early-stage disease
